title: COSC445 - Design and Analysis of Algorithms
text:
Section: 01
Credit hours: 4
Term and year: Spring 2026
Delivery Mode Classroom
When and Where: Tuesday, Thursday. 1:45 PM - 3:00 PM. Schaefer Hall, Room 165.
Instructor: Sayan Goswami
Office: Schaefer Hall, Room 152
Office Hours: Monday and Wednesday from 3:00 PM to 4:00 PM or by appointment.
Phone: 240-895-2153
Email: sgoswami@smcm.edu
Textbook: The Algorithm Design Manual, 3rd Edition, by Steven Skiena
Prerequisites: COSC 201; and MATH 200 or MATH 281



This course studies the design, implementation and analysis of important algorithms. Topics include lower and upper complexity bounds, algorithm analysis techniques, NP-completeness, sorting algorithms, searching algorithms, graph algorithms, divide-and-conquer algorithms, greedy algorithms, dynamic programming, backtracking, probabilistic algorithms, and mathematical algorithms.



By the end of this course, you will be able to:

Analyze the best-case, average-case, and worst-case asymptotic performance of algorithms using standard notations (Big-O, \(\Omega\), \(\Theta\)).
Construct correctness proofs for algorithms using techniques such as loop invariants and mathematical induction.
Compare and contrast major algorithms and data structures (such as sorting, graph traversal, and hashing) to determine their suitability for specific computational problems.
Apply key algorithmic design paradigms, including divide-and-conquer, dynamic programming, and greedy strategies, to solve complex problems.
Synthesize efficient algorithms for engineering design situations and validate their performance through theoretical analysis and empirical testing.
Categorize computational problems into complexity classes (P, NP, NP-Complete) and identify when a problem is likely to be computationally intractable.




The Engaged Learning component will include problem solving and readings.

Your course grade will be based on homework assignments, quizzes, a midterm exam, and a final exam. All components will emphasize algorithm design and problem-solving.

Homework Assignments: Homework is the primary vehicle for learning in this course. These sets will involve:

Algorithm Design: Developing efficient solutions to complex engineering problems.
Proofs: Providing mathematical justification (e.g., loop invariants, induction) for algorithm correctness.
Complexity Analysis: Determining the asymptotic bounds (e.g., \(O(n \log n)\)) of your proposed solutions.
Note: Submission must be through pdf files, and must be typed (not hand written). Using Markdown with LaTeX is highly encouraged to ensure clarity in mathematical notation.

Quizzes Quizzes are short, low-stakes assessments designed to ensure you are keeping up with core concepts. These will focus on “familiarity” tasks, such as tracing a specific algorithm (like Dijkstra’s or Quicksort) on a provided data set or identifying the complexity class of a given function.
Midterm Exam and Final Exam Exams will test your ability to synthesize the paradigms learned in class under time constraints.

The Midterm covers foundational analysis, sorting, and divide-and-conquer.
The Final Exam is cumulative but will heavily emphasize dynamic programming, greedy algorithms, and graph theory.


Important: All quizzes and exams will be paper-based.

title: A* Search
text:
Where We’ve Been
You already know:

Backtracking — explore, undo, try another way
Pruning — skip branches that can’t possibly work


Question: In backtracking, when you have multiple branches to try — which one do you try first?


Usually… whichever comes first. Arbitrary order.


What if we could rank our choices and always try the most promising one?
That’s the core idea behind A*.

title: A* Search
text:
Motivating Example: Navigation
You’re in a city. You want to go from A to B.


Option 1 — Blind search:
Expand every reachable node equally.
Eventually you’ll find B… maybe.



A broad, wasteful search frontier spreading in all directions



Option 2 — Guided search:
Prefer nodes that are closer to B and cheaply reached.



A more focussed elliptical search frontier growing towards the destination





Recap: In backtracking, when you have multiple branches to try — which one do you try first?


Its arbitrary. A* gives us a principled way to rank our choices and always pick the best one first.

title: A* Search
text:
Two Algorithms to Know First
Before A*, let’s name two simpler cousins:



Algorithm
Strategy
Blind to…




BFS
Expand shallowest node first
Cost of edges


Dijkstra’s
Expand cheapest-cost node first
Direction to goal


A*
Expand best estimated total cost
Nothing — uses both!





A* = Dijkstra’s + a compass pointing toward the goal.

title: A* Search
text:
The Key Insight: Two Costs
For every node \(n\) we’re considering, A* tracks:
\[f(n) = g(n) + h(n)\]


\(g(n)\)
Cost so far
How much did it cost to reach this node from the start?
(exact — we know this)

\(h(n)\)
Estimated cost ahead
How far are we still from the goal?
(a guess — a heuristic)

\(f(n)\)
Total estimated cost
Our best estimate of the whole path cost through \(n\).
(what A* uses to decide)



Question: \(g\) is exact. \(h\) is a guess. Why not just use \(g\) and be precise?

title: A* Search
section: The Heuristic \(h(n)\): A Guess With Rules
text:
The Heuristic \(h(n)\): A Guess With Rules
\(h(n)\) is an estimate of the cost from \(n\) to the goal.
You get to design it — but it must follow one key rule.
Admissibility rule: \(h(n)\) must never overestimate the true cost.
\[h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \text{(true cost to goal)}\]

Question: Why would overestimating be a problem?
Think: if \(h\) overestimates, what might A* skip?


It might skip the optimal path — mistakenly thinking it’s too expensive.

title: A* Search
text:
Classic Heuristic: Straight-Line Distance
For navigation on a map, a natural heuristic is:
\[h(n) = \text{straight-line distance from } n \text{ to goal}\]
Question: Is straight-line distance admissible? Can the real road ever be shorter than a straight line?

No! Roads can only be at least as long as the straight line.
So straight-line distance never overestimates. ✅ Admissible.


Admissibility is easy to reason about intuitively:
“Could the real cost ever be less than my guess?”
If the answer is no, you’re admissible.

title: A* Search
text:
How A* Works: The Algorithm
def search(graph):
  open_set = priority_queue()  # ordered by f(n)
  start = graph.start_node
  f[start] = g[start] + h[start]
  open_set.add(start, f[start])  # add the start node with priority value f

  while open_set is not empty:
    node = open_set.pop()  # get the node with lowest f(n)
    if node == goal:
      return reconstruct_path(node) # found goal! reconstruct path back to source
    for neighbor in graph.neighbors(node):
      if neighbor not in open_set:
        g[neighbor] = g[node] + cost(node, neighbor)
        f[neighbor] = g[neighbor] + h[neighbor]
        open_set.add(neighbor, f[neighbor])
  return FAILURE

Notice: we only update a node if we found a better path to it.
Question: What does this remind you of from earlier in the course?

Z (old)
—
—
449


T (old)
—
—
447



Best next: R (\(f = 413\))





Question: We’re skipping Z and T for now, even though they’re direct neighbors of the start.
Is this a problem? Could we be missing the optimal path?

title: A* Search
section: Why A* Doesn’t Miss the Optimal Path
text:
Why A* Doesn’t Miss the Optimal Path

Key guarantee: If \(h\) is admissible, A* will find the optimal path.

Intuition:

The optimal path has some true cost \(C^*\)
Any unexplored node on the optimal path has \(f \leq C^*\)
(because \(h\) never overestimates what remains)
A* only stops when it pops the goal from the queue
When the goal is popped, its \(g\) value is the optimal cost



Question: What would happen if we used \(h = 0\) for everything?
(i.e., we pretend there’s no remaining cost)



\(h = 0\) → \(f = g\) → A* becomes Dijkstra’s! Still correct, just slower.

title: A* Search
text:
Heuristic Quality Matters
All admissible heuristics give correct answers.
But better heuristics give faster answers.

Question: Which heuristic explores fewer nodes — a good one or a weak one?


A stronger \(h\) (closer to the true cost) → fewer nodes expanded → faster.



Heuristic
Behavior




\(h = 0\)
Dijkstra’s — explores many nodes


\(h = h^*\) (perfect)
Only explores nodes on the optimal path


\(h > h^*\) (inadmissible)
Fast, but may miss optimal






The sweet spot: as close to \(h^*\) as possible without going over.

title: A* Search
text:
Designing Heuristics: Relaxed Problems
A powerful technique: solve an easier version of the problem.

“What if some constraints didn’t exist?”


8-Puzzle example: move tiles to reach goal configuration.


START               GOAL

+---+---+---+       +---+---+---+
| 7 | 2 | 4 |       | 1 | 2 | 3 |
+---+---+---+       +---+---+---+
| 5 |   | 6 |       | 4 | 5 | 6 |
+---+---+---+       +---+---+---+
| 8 | 3 | 1 |       | 7 | 8 |   |
+---+---+---+       +---+---+---+

Possible heuristics:

\(h_1\): Misplaced tiles — count tiles not in their goal position
(Relaxation: tiles can teleport anywhere)
\(h_2\): Manhattan distance — sum of how far each tile needs to move
(Relaxation: tiles can pass through each other)






Question: Which is stronger, \(h_1\) or \(h_2\)?
Can you give an example where they differ?

title: A* Search
text:
Dominance: Comparing Heuristics
We say \(h_2\) dominates \(h_1\) if:
\[h_2(n) \geq h_1(n) \quad \text{for all } n\]
(and both are admissible)

A dominating heuristic always expands no more nodes than the dominated one.
Manhattan distance dominates misplaced tiles for the 8-puzzle.



Question: If you have two admissible heuristics \(h_1\) and \(h_2\), can you combine them?
How?



Yes! \(h(n) = \max(h_1(n), h_2(n))\) is admissible and dominates both.

title: A* Search
text:
Consistency (a Stronger Property)
Admissibility: \(h(n) \leq\) true cost from \(n\) to goal.
Consistency (also called monotonicity): for every edge \(n \to m\):
\[h(n) \leq \text{cost}(n \to m) + h(m)\]

Question: What does this inequality look like geometrically?
(Hint: think triangle)


It’s the triangle inequality — the heuristic can’t drop faster than the actual edge cost.

Consistency → Admissibility (but not vice versa).
With a consistent heuristic, every node is expanded at most once — no re-expansion!

title: A* Search
text:
A* vs. Dijkstra’s: Visualized


Dijkstra’s (\(h = 0\))
Expands in uniform rings of equal cost — like ripples in a pond.
Explores in all directions equally.


A* (good \(h\))
Expands in an ellipse stretching toward the goal.
Focuses exploration where it matters.




Question: When would A* and Dijkstra’s expand the same set of nodes?



When the goal is the closest node — or when \(h\) gives no useful information about direction.

text:
Section: 01
Credit hours: 4

Delivery Mode Classroom
Instructor: Sayan Goswami
Office: Schaefer Hall, Room 152

This course examines object-oriented design and programming in greater detail. An examination of the use of the data structures provided by language-specific libraries, and some common simple algorithms. Students learn to design and implement simple applications. The emphasis of this course is on learning good design. This course is suitable for non-majors who want to improve their ability to solve problems using programs.



By the end of this course, you will be able to:

Design and implement object-oriented programs in Java that integrate encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and interfaces.
Analyze and debug Java programs to trace execution, identify errors, and improve correctness.
Evaluate alternative design choices (e.g., composition vs inheritance, abstract classes vs interfaces) and justify trade-offs.
Create a medium-scale Java project that demonstrates sound object-oriented design principles.




The Engaged Learning component will emphasize teamwork in solving programming problems. Together in class, we will finalize the format of this activity, and with my guidance, you will help choose the problems your teams will work on.



I support the SMCM Code of Student Conduct, which states, in part: “St. Mary’s College of Maryland is committed to the ideals of honesty, personal integrity, and mutual trust.  Academic integrity is a responsibility of all students, members of the faculty, and administrative officers.  All students are expected to uphold the highest ideals of academic integrity throughout their career at St. Mary’s.”
Students who commit acts of academic misconduct are subject to in-class penalties imposed by the instructor and to a hearing before the Academic Judicial Board with possibilities of additional penalties. 
You are responsible for understanding and complying with the SMCM Code of Student Conduct.



SMCM is committed to providing access to the learning and living experience to students with disabilities and disabling health conditions. If you have received a letter from the Office of Accessibility Services (OAS), which outlines the academic accommodations to which you are entitled to and you want those accommodations to apply to this course, you MUST share your letter and meet with me to review that letter. If you suspect that you have a learning or living need related to a disability or disabling health condition that could benefit from accommodations, you should contact the Office of Accessibility Services, who can help you learn more about how to proceed. Digital accessibility requirements under Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act go into effect as of April 24, 2026. The instructor is making every reasonable effort to ensure that the digital materials distributed to students and/or required to engage in the course are reviewed for compliance with the federal regulations. If the issue has not been resolved within one week, please report this accessibility issue by way of the College’s standing Campus Accessibility Issue reporting form for review by our ADA Coordinator.

For any other help call 240-895-4388, stop by Glendening 230, or email os3@smcm.edu .



St. Mary’s College of Maryland is committed to helping create a safe and open learning environment for all students. If you (or someone you know) have experienced any form of sexual misconduct, including sexual assault, dating or domestic violence, or stalking, know that help and support are available.
The College strongly encourages all community members to take action, seek support, and report incidents of sexual misconduct to the Title IX Office.  Under Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972, I am required to disclose information about such misconduct to the Title IX Office.
If you would like to talk to a confidential employee who does not have this reporting responsibility, you can contact SMCM Wellness Center (240) 895-4289. 
For more information about reporting options and resources at St. Mary’s College of Maryland and the community, please visit the Office of Title IX Compliance and Training.

Doing what you can to stay ahead by wisely taking care of yourself will be a key to succeeding academically.  Sometimes mental health challenges can affect your ability to complete required work. For example, a particular assignment might trigger anxiety for you in ways which were not anticipated. Or, maybe it becomes difficult to attend class due to mental health challenges. The sooner you share your challenges with me, the better prepared I am to assist you.  I am one of many people here at SMCM who care about you and your welfare. To learn more about Wellness Center Services please go to their webpage at https://www.smcm.edu/wellness/

title: Java File I/O & Persistence
text:
What is Persistence?
When your program ends, all variables and objects disappear from memory.
Persistence means saving data so it survives after the program closes.
Without persistence:          With persistence:
┌─────────────┐               ┌─────────────┐
│  ArrayList  │  ← LOST       │  ArrayList  │ ──save──▶ students.csv
│  [Alice]    │  when         │  [Alice]    │           (lives forever)
│  [Bob]      │  program      │  [Bob]      │ ◀─load──  (reload anytime)
└─────────────┘  ends         └─────────────┘
Common ways to persist data:

Text files (.txt)
CSV files (.csv) ← we focus on this
Binary files
Databases (SQL)

title: Java File I/O & Persistence
section: The Console I/O You Already Know

title: Java File I/O & Persistence
section: The Console I/O You Already Know
text:
The Console I/O You Already Know
The key insight for this whole unit:

File I/O works exactly the same way — you just swap the source and destination.

Console:  Scanner(System.in)      →  read from keyboard
File:     Scanner(new File(...))  →  read from file

Console:  System.out.println()    →  write to screen
File:     PrintWriter.println()   →  write to file
Same methods. Different destination. That’s it.

Java File I/O & Persistence
text:
CSV Files
CSV = Comma-Separated Values
A plain text file where each line is a record, and fields are separated by commas.
name,age,major
Alice,20,Computer Science
Bob,22,Mathematics
Carol,21,Physics

Line 1 is usually a header row (column names)
Each subsequent line is one data record
Commas separate the fields within each record

CSV is one of the most universal data formats — Excel, Google Sheets, databases, and virtually every programming language can read and write it.

section: Parsing a CSV Line with split()
text:
Parsing a CSV Line with split()
Before reading objects back, we need to understand split():
String line = "Alice,20,3.8";
String[] parts = line.split(",");

System.out.println(parts[0]);  // Alice
System.out.println(parts[1]);  // 20
System.out.println(parts[2]);  // 3.8
split(",") breaks a string into an array at every comma.
Type conversion — everything in a CSV is text, so convert back manually:
String name  = parts[0];
int    age   = Integer.parseInt(parts[1]);    // String → int
double gpa   = Double.parseDouble(parts[2]);  // String → double

section: Putting It All Together — A Rudimentary Database
text:
Putting It All Together — A Rudimentary Database
Here’s what we just built:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Your Java Program                    │
│                                                         │
│  ArrayList<Student>  ──── write ────▶  students.csv     │
│                                            │            │
│  ArrayList<Student>  ◀─── read  ────       │            │
│       (rebuilt)                       (persisted)       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
This is exactly how simple databases work:

Save state → serialize objects to a file
Load state → deserialize the file back into objects
The file outlives the program → your data is persistent

Real databases (MySQL, SQLite) do the same thing under the hood — they just do it faster, with more features, and with proper querying support.

text:
Common Errors & How to Fix Them








Error
Cause
Fix




FileNotFoundException
File path doesn’t exist when reading
Check the filename; use f.exists() first


IOException
General I/O failure
Wrap in try-catch, check disk space


ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException in split()
Line has fewer commas than expected
Check for blank lines or malformed rows


NumberFormatException
parseInt() on a non-numeric string
Verify column order; check for spaces with trim()


File is empty after writing
Forgot to call .close()
Use try-with-resources


New data overwrites old data
Default FileWriter overwrites
Pass true for append mode

section: Quick Reference — File I/O Cheat Sheet

title: Java File I/O & Persistence
text:
Summary

Persistence means saving data beyond a program’s lifetime
The File class represents a file path and lets you check existence, get metadata, and manage files before opening them
File I/O is just console I/O redirected to a file — the same Scanner and PrintWriter methods apply
Use PrintWriter + FileWriter to write — same println() and printf() you already know
Use Scanner + new File(...) to read — same nextLine() and hasNextLine() you already know
Always use try-with-resources so files close automatically
CSV stores objects as comma-separated lines of text — add a toCSV() method to your class
Use split(",") and type conversion to rebuild objects when reading
BufferedReader is faster for large files and common in professional code — same concept, slightly different syntax
This read/write cycle forms a rudimentary persistent database

Part 1 — The Student Class
Create a Student class with the following fields:



Field
Type




name
String


id
int


major
String


gpa
double



Tasks:

Write the constructor, getters, and a toString() method.
Write a toCSV() method that returns a string in the format:
name,id,major,gpa
Example output for one student: Alice,1001,CS,3.85

title: Java File I/O & Persistence
text:
Part 2 — Write to CSV
Write a method with this signature:
public static void saveStudents(ArrayList<Student> students, String filename) throws IOException
This method should:

Open filename for writing using PrintWriter and FileWriter
Write a header row: name,id,major,gpa
Loop through the ArrayList and write each student using toCSV()
Use try-with-resources so the file closes automatically

Test it by creating at least 4 students, calling saveStudents(), and opening the resulting CSV in a text editor to verify it looks correct.

title: Java File I/O & Persistence
text:
Part 3 — Read From CSV
Write a method with this signature:
public static ArrayList<Student> loadStudents(String filename) throws FileNotFoundException
This method should:

Create an empty ArrayList<Student>
Use File and f.exists() to check the file exists before opening — print a message and return the empty list if it doesn’t
Open the file for reading using Scanner and new File(...)
Skip the header line with one call to nextLine()
Read each remaining line, split by comma, parse each field to the correct type, and construct a Student object
Add each Student to the ArrayList and return it

title: Java File I/O & Persistence
Java File I/O & Persistence
text:
Challenge Extensions

Add a student from user input — use Scanner(System.in) to prompt for a new student’s details, append them to the CSV using append mode (new FileWriter("students.csv", true)), then reload and print the full list.
Search the database — after loading, write a method that takes a String major and returns an ArrayList<Student> of only students in that major.
Sort before saving — use Collections.sort() with a Comparator to sort students by GPA (descending) before writing the CSV.
Handle bad data — add a try-catch inside your loadStudents() loop so that one malformed line doesn’t crash the whole load. Print a warning and skip the bad line instead.
Use the File class — before saving, use File to check if students.csv already exists. If it does, print how many bytes the old file was before overwriting it.

text:
Step 1: Install the Tools
You need two main things installed on your computer:

Java JDK (Version 17 or higher): Download here. (Make sure to pick the installer for your OS: Windows, macOS, or Linux).
Git:

Windows: Download and run the Git for Windows installer. Just click “Next” on all defaults.
macOS: Open your terminal and type git --version. If it’s not there, it will ask you to install “Xcode Command Line Tools.” Say yes.

VS Code: Download here.

title: COSC130 - Project Setup
text:
Step 2: Get the Code
We aren’t going to download a ZIP file. Instead, we are going to “Clone” the project so you can receive updates from me later.

Open VS Code.
Press Ctrl + Shift + P (Windows) or Cmd + Shift + P (Mac) to open the Command Palette.
Type Git: Clone and press Enter.
Paste this URL: https://github.com/sayangoswami/ezui.git
Select a folder on your computer to save the project.
When prompted, click Open in the bottom right corner.

Step 3: Setup Java in VS Code
Once the project is open, VS Code will likely show a pop-up in the bottom right saying: “The ‘Extension Pack for Java’ is recommended for this repository.”

Click Install. This gives you the “Play” button to run your code easily.

If it doesn’t pop up, you might already have it. Go to the Extensions view (Ctrl + Shift + X or Cmd + Shift + X) and search for “Extension Pack for Java”.

text:
Step 4: How to write your code
To keep things simple and ensure your project doesn’t break when I send updates:

Your Code: Create your new Java files inside the src/ folder.
The Library: Do not modify any files inside the lib/ or src/ezui/ folders.

title: COSC130 - Project Setup
text:
Step 5: Getting Updates from me
Sometimes I will fix a bug or add a new feature to the EZUI library. To get these changes without losing your work, follow these three steps in the VS Code Terminal (shortcut: Ctrl + `).

Save your current spot: git stash (This “hides” your current changes temporarily so they don’t get in the way of the update.)
Download the update: git pull origin main
Bring your work back: git stash pop (This puts your code back exactly where it was, now with my updates included!)

In VS Code, the shortcut to toggle (open/hide) the terminal is:

Windows & Linux
Ctrl + ` (Backtick)


macOS
⌃ (Control) + ` (Backtick)

Note: The “Backtick” key is usually located in the top-left corner of the keyboard, right below the Esc key and above the Tab key.

Since you are using VS Code, you will have access to the Timeline View (usually at the bottom of the File Explorer). - VS Code keeps a “Local History” of every save. - If you mess up a git pull or delete a file accidentally, you can restore it from the Timeline without knowing advanced Git commands.

Red squiggly lines: Ensure you installed the “Java Extension Pack” and that you have opened the entire folder (ezui) in VS Code, not just a single file.

title: Introduction to Exceptions
text:
Introduction to Exceptions

What is an Exception? An event that occurs during the execution of a program that disrupts the normal flow of instructions.
The Philosophy: In CS1, we assume everything works. In CS2, we assume things will break.

Exceptions allow us to separate “Error-Handling Code” from “Regular Logic.”

Terminology:

Throwing: When an error occurs, the JVM creates an Exception Object and “throws” it.
Catching: If you have a handler ready, you “catch” the object and deal with it.
Crashing: If no one catches the exception, the program terminates (the “Stack Trace” of death).

title: Introduction to Exceptions
text:
The Exception Hierarchy (Visual Map)

Throwable: The parent of all things that can be thrown.

Error: Serious problems that a reasonable application should not try to catch (e.g., StackOverflowError, OutOfMemoryError). These are system-level failures.
Exception: The class we focus on.

RuntimeException (Unchecked): Usually caused by programmer logic errors. (e.g., NullPointerException, IndexOutOfBoundsException).
IO/Other (Checked): Caused by external factors. Java forces you to acknowledge these.

title: Introduction to Exceptions
text:
Checked vs. Unchecked Exceptions








Feature
Unchecked (RuntimeException)
Checked (Exception)




Cause
Bad logic (e.g., dividing by zero).
External environment (e.g., file missing).


Compiler
Does not check. Program runs until it hits the error.
Mandatory. The code won’t compile unless handled.


Example
NullPointerException
IOException, SQLException


Solution
Fix your logic (use an if statement).
Use a try-catch or throws.

title: Introduction to Exceptions
text:
The Try-Catch-Finally Mechanism

Try: Contains the “Risky Code.” If an exception occurs here, the JVM stops executing the rest of the try block and jumps immediately to the catch.
Catch: Defines the type of exception you are looking for. You can have multiple catch blocks for different errors.
Finally: Code that must run, whether an exception happened or not. Used for “cleanup” (like closing a database connection or a file).

Note: Even if there is a return statement in the try, the finally block still executes before the method finishes!

title: Introduction to Exceptions
Introduction to Exceptions
text:
The “Throws” Keyword (Passing the Hot Potato)

Sometimes a method shouldn’t fix the error; it should just warn the caller that an error might happen.
Syntax: public void readFile() throws IOException { ... }
If Method A calls Method B, and Method B throws an exception, Method A must either:

Wrap the call in a try-catch.
Also declare throws in its own header, passing the responsibility up the chain to the main method.

title: Introduction to Exceptions
section: Defining and Throwing Your Own Exceptions

title: Introduction to Exceptions
text:
Best Practices & Common Pitfalls

Don’t Swallow Exceptions: Avoid catch(Exception e) {}. If you do this, your program fails silently, and you’ll spend hours debugging a problem you hid from yourself.
Use getMessage(): Every exception object has a getMessage() method. Print it! It tells you exactly what went wrong.
Don’t use Exceptions for Flow Control: Don’t use a try-catch to end a loop. Use standard if statements and boolean logic for normal behavior. Exceptions are for exceptional (unplanned) events.

title: Introduction to Exceptions
Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
What Is Dynamic Programming?
Dynamic Programming (DP) is a technique for solving problems by:

Breaking them into overlapping subproblems
Solving each subproblem once
Storing the result so it’s never recomputed




DP is NOT:

A data structure
A specific algorithm
Only for hard problems




DP IS:

A way of thinking about recursion
An optimisation of repeated work
Recursion + memory





One-liner: “Remember what you’ve already solved.”

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
The Two Ingredients of DP
Every DP problem requires exactly two things:



1. Optimal Substructure
The optimal solution to the problem contains optimal solutions to its subproblems.

“I can build the best answer from the best answers to smaller versions.”




2. Overlapping Subproblems
The same subproblems appear again and again during recursion.

“I keep solving the same smaller problems repeatedly — let me cache them.”




If both conditions hold → DP will help you.

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
DP vs. Divide & Conquer




Divide & Conquer
Dynamic Programming




Subproblems
Independent
Overlapping


Reuse results?
No (not needed)
Yes (essential)


Examples
Merge Sort, Binary Search
Fibonacci, Knapsack


Overhead
Recursion only
Recursion + memory





Merge Sort splits [1..n] into [1..n/2] and [n/2+1..n]. Those halves never overlap.
Fibonacci needs fib(4) to compute both fib(5) and fib(6). They do overlap.

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
Why Is It So Slow?
The call tree for fib(6) has 25 nodes, even though there are only 7 distinct subproblems (fib(0)…fib(6)).

fib(6) call treefib(3) alone is called 3 times, fib(2) is called 5 times.

This is the overlapping subproblems problem.
The fix? Store results the first time. Memoisation.

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
Fibonacci — Top-Down DP (Memoisation)
Add a cache. If we’ve seen it before, return the stored answer.
memo = {}
def fib_memo(n):
    if n in memo:
        return memo[n]          # ← cache hit!
    if n <= 1:
        return n

    memo[n] = fib_memo(n-1) + fib_memo(n-2)  # ← store result in cache
    return memo[n]
Now fib(5) only ever computes each value once:
fib(5) → fib(4) → fib(3) → fib(2) → fib(1) = 1
                                   → fib(0) = 0
                         → fib(1) = 1  [cached: skip]
               → fib(2) = 1  [cached: skip]
         → fib(3) = 2  [cached: skip]
Time: O(n) — Space: O(n)

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
Fibonacci — Bottom-Up DP (Tabulation)
Instead of recursing top-down, fill a table from the base cases upward.
def fib_dp(n):
    if n <= 1:
        return n

    dp = [0] * (n + 1)
    dp[0] = 0
    dp[1] = 1

    for i in range(2, n + 1):
        dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]   # ← use already-computed values

    return dp[n]

Time: O(n) — Space: O(n) (can reduce to O(1) with two variables)

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
Two Flavours of DP









Top-Down (Memoisation)
Bottom-Up (Tabulation)




Style
Recursive + cache
Iterative + table


Order
Lazy (computed on demand)
Explicit (fill in order)


Easier to
Write (mirrors recurrence)
Optimise (no call stack)


Risk
Stack overflow for large n
Need to figure out fill order


Use when
Not all subproblems needed
All subproblems needed




Both give the same asymptotic complexity.
Start with top-down (it’s easier), then convert to bottom-up if needed.

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
Problem: Climbing Stairs

You’re climbing a staircase with n steps.
Each move you can climb 1 or 2 steps.
How many distinct ways can you reach the top?


Examples:

n = 1: [1] → 1 way
n = 2: [1+1], [2] → 2 ways
n = 3: [1+1+1], [1+2], [2+1] → 3 ways
n = 4: [1+1+1+1], [1+1+2], [1+2+1], [2+1+1], [2+2] → 5 ways

Notice anything?


1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ... — it’s Fibonacci!

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
Climbing Stairs — Recurrence
Key insight: to reach step n, you came from either:

step n-1 (took a 1-step), or
step n-2 (took a 2-step)

So: ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2)

def climb_stairs(n):
    if n <= 2:
        return n

    dp = [0] * (n + 1)
    dp[1] = 1   # one way to reach step 1
    dp[2] = 2   # two ways to reach step 2

    for i in range(3, n + 1):
        dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]

    return dp[n]


This problem teaches the core DP pattern: define dp[i] = answer for subproblem of size i, build up from base cases.

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
Problem: Coin Change

You have coins of denominations [1, 5, 6, 9].
What is the minimum number of coins to make amount n = 11?

Greedy fails here! Greedy picks 9 first → 9 + 1 + 1 = 3 coins.
But optimal is 5 + 6 = 2 coins. ✓
Why DP? To make amount n, we can use any coin c, and then we need to make n - c with minimum coins. The subproblems overlap heavily.

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
Coin Change — Recurrence
Let dp[i] = minimum coins needed to make amount i.
Recurrence: \[dp[i] = 1 + \min_{c \in \text{coins}} dp[i - c] \quad \text{for } i \geq c\]
Base case: dp[0] = 0 (zero coins needed to make 0)
Intuition: for each amount, try every coin and take the best option.

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
Coin Change — Code
def coin_change(coins, amount):
    INF = float('inf')
    dp = [INF] * (amount + 1)
    dp[0] = 0   # base case

    for i in range(1, amount + 1):
        for coin in coins:
            if coin <= i and dp[i - coin] != INF:
                dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i - coin] + 1)

    return dp[amount] if dp[amount] != INF else -1

print(coin_change([1, 5, 6, 9], 11))  # → 2
print(coin_change([2], 3))            # → -1 (impossible)
Time: O(amount × |coins|)
Space: O(amount)

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
Problem: Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)

Given two strings, find the length of their longest common subsequence.

A subsequence keeps characters in order but skips some.
Example:
s1 = "ABCBDAB"
s2 = "BDCABA"

LCS = "BCBA"  (length 4)
B_C_B_A appears in both strings (in order, not necessarily contiguous).
Applications: diff tools (git diff!), DNA sequence alignment, spell checking.

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
LCS — Recurrence
Define dp[i][j] = LCS length of s1[0..i-1] and s2[0..j-1].
Recurrence:
\(dp[i][j] = \begin{cases} dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 & \text{if } s1[i-1] = s2[j-1] \\ \max(dp[i-1][j],\ dp[i][j-1]) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\)
Intuition:

Characters match → extend the LCS by 1
Characters don’t match → take the better of skipping from either string

Base cases: dp[0][j] = 0, dp[i][0] = 0 (empty string has no common subsequence)

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
LCS — Filling the Table
\(dp[i][j] = \begin{cases} dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 & \text{if } s1[i-1] = s2[j-1] \\ \max(dp[i-1][j],\ dp[i][j-1]) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\)


Sequence 1: C T A G T C A Sequence 2: A G C T A C G

Each cell only looks at its left, top, and diagonal neighbour.
dp[0][j] = 0, dp[i][0] = 0





dp table for LCS

Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
Problem: 0/1 Knapsack

You have a knapsack with capacity W.
There are n items, each with a weight and a value.
Maximise total value without exceeding capacity.
Each item is either taken (1) or left (0).

Example: capacity W = 7



Item
Weight
Value




A
1
1


B
3
4


C
4
5


D
5
7



Best choice: B + C = weight 7, value 9 ✓

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
Knapsack — Recurrence
Define dp[i][w] = max value using the first i items with capacity w.
For each item i, two choices:
\[
dp[i][w] = \begin{cases} dp[i-1][w] & \text{skip item } i \\ dp[i-1][w - wt_i] + val_i & \text{take item } i \quad (\text{if } wt_i \leq w) \end{cases}
\]
Take the maximum of the two options.
Base case: dp[0][w] = 0 for all w (no items → no value)

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
Knapsack — Filling the Table
\[
dp[i][w] = \begin{cases} dp[i-1][w] & \text{skip item } i \\ dp[i-1][w - wt_i] + val_i & \text{take item } i \quad (\text{if } wt_i \leq w) \end{cases}
\]

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
Knapsack — Code
def knapsack(weights, values, W):
    n = len(weights)
    dp = [[0] * (W + 1) for _ in range(n + 1)]

    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        wt, val = weights[i-1], values[i-1]
        for w in range(W + 1):
            # Option 1: skip item i
            dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w]

            # Option 2: take item i (if it fits)
            if wt <= w:
                dp[i][w] = max(dp[i][w], dp[i-1][w - wt] + val)

    return dp[n][W]

weights = [1, 3, 4, 5]
values  = [1, 4, 5, 7]
print(knapsack(weights, values, 7))   # → 9
Time: O(n × W) — Space: O(n × W)

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
The DP Design Process
When you see a new problem, ask these questions in order:

Can I define the problem recursively?
→ “What smaller versions of this problem do I need?”
Do subproblems overlap?
→ Draw the recursion tree. Are the same calls repeated?
What is my dp[...] state?
→ What information fully describes a subproblem?
What is the recurrence?
→ How does dp[i] (or dp[i][j]) depend on smaller states?
What are the base cases?
→ What are the smallest subproblems I can answer directly?
In what order should I fill the table?
→ Each cell must be computed after the cells it depends on.

title: Introduction to Dynamic Programming
text:
Identifying the State
The state is the hardest part. It must capture everything you need to solve a subproblem — no more, no less.








Problem
State
Meaning




Fibonacci
dp[i]
F(i)


Climbing Stairs
dp[i]
ways to reach step i


Coin Change
dp[i]
min coins for amount i


LCS
dp[i][j]
LCS of s1[0..i-1] and s2[0..j-1]


Knapsack
dp[i][w]
max value using first i items, capacity w




Rule of thumb: if the problem has one “variable dimension” → 1D table.
If it has two → 2D table. Three → 3D. (Rare beyond 3D.)

title: Combinatorial Search
text:
What Is Combinatorial Search?
Many problems ask: “Find a solution among a huge set of candidates.”


Examples:

Arrange 8 queens on a chessboard so none attack each other
Find a path through a maze
Schedule meetings with no conflicts
Solve a Sudoku puzzle
Colour a map with 4 colours


The naive plan:
for every possible candidate:
    if it is a valid solution:
        return it
Problem: the candidate space is astronomically large.

title: Combinatorial Search
text:
Why Brute Force Fails
For n-Queens (placing n non-attacking queens):



n
Arrangements to check




8
4,426,165,368


10
~3.6 billion


15
~1.3 trillion


20
~6 × 10¹⁷




Even at 10⁹ checks/second, n=20 would take ~20 years.

We need a smarter strategy.

title: Combinatorial Search
text:
The Core Insight: Build Incrementally
Instead of generating complete candidates, build solutions step by step.
At every step:

Extend the partial solution by one choice
Check immediately — is this partial solution still valid?
If not valid → abandon it entirely (backtrack!)
If valid → keep going

This is backtracking: systematic exploration with early abandonment.

title: Combinatorial Search
Combinatorial Search
text:
Example 1: The Maze
Problem: Find a path from Start (S) to End (E) in a grid.
S . . #
# # . #
. . . .
# # # E
Choices at each step: move Up, Down, Left, or Right
Constraint: stay in bounds, don’t hit #, don’t revisit cells
Backtrack when: we hit a dead end with no valid moves

title: Combinatorial Search
text:
Maze — Step-by-Step Trace
S . . #
# # . #
. . . .
# # # E
Start: [(0,0)]

→ Try Right: (0,1) ✓  →  Path: [(0,0),(0,1)]
  → Try Right: (0,2) ✓  →  Path: [(0,0),(0,1),(0,2)]
    → Try Right: (0,3) = '#'  ✗
    → Try Down: (1,2) ✓  →  Path: [...,(1,2)]
      → Try Down: (2,2) ✓  →  Path: [...,(2,2)]
        → Try Right: (2,3) ✓  →  Path: [...,(2,3)]
          → Try Down: (3,3) = 'E' ✓  DONE!
Simple idea, powerful result. The key is the undo on dead ends.

title: Combinatorial Search
Combinatorial Search
section: Example 2: Subsets That Sum to a Target
text:
Example 2: Subsets That Sum to a Target
Problem: Given [3, 1, 4, 2, 5], find all subsets that sum to 6.
Approach: At each position, decide include or skip the element.

backtrack(index=0, current_sum=0, chosen=[])
├── include 3  → sum=3
│   ├── include 1  → sum=4
│   │   ├── include 4  → sum=8  ✗ (over target)
│   │   ├── skip    4
│   │   │   ├── include 2  → sum=6  ✓ [3,1,2]
│   │   │   └── skip 2
│   │   │       └── include 5 → sum=9 ✗
│   │   └── ...
│   └── skip 1
│       ├── include 4  → sum=7  ✗
│       └── skip 4
│           ├── include 2  → sum=5
│           │   └── include 5 → sum=10 ✗
│           └── skip 2
│               └── include 5 → sum=8 ✗
└── skip 3  → ...  (finds [1,5])

title: Combinatorial Search
text:
Subset Sum — Code
def subset_sum(nums, target):
    results = []

    def explore(index, current_sum, chosen):
        if current_sum == target:
            results.append(chosen[:])  # found a valid subset!
            return
        if index == len(nums) or current_sum > target:
            return                     # dead end

        # Choice 1: include nums[index]
        chosen.append(nums[index])
        explore(index + 1, current_sum + nums[index], chosen)
        chosen.pop()  # ← undo

        # Choice 2: skip nums[index]
        explore(index + 1, current_sum, chosen)
    explore(0, 0, [])
    return results

print(subset_sum([3, 1, 4, 2, 5], 6))
# → [[3, 1, 2], [1, 5], [4, 2]]

title: Combinatorial Search
text:
Example 3: N-Queens
Problem: Place n queens on an n×n board so no two queens share a row, column, or diagonal.
Key observations:

Place one queen per row (so row index = recursion depth)
Track which columns and diagonals are taken

Row 0:  Q . . .    Q placed in col 0
Row 1:  . . Q .    col 0 and diagonals blocked → col 2 is free
Row 2:  . . . .    cols 0,2 and diagonals blocked → ...
Row 3:  . Q . .    eventually col 1 works!

title: Combinatorial Search
text:
N-Queens — Code
def n_queens(n):
    solutions = []
    cols = set()
    diag1 = set()   # row - col (top-left to bottom-right)
    diag2 = set()   # row + col (top-right to bottom-left)

    def explore(row, placement):
        if row == n:
            solutions.append(placement[:])
            return

        for col in range(n):
            if col in cols or (row-col) in diag1 or (row+col) in diag2:
                continue   # ← this column/diagonal is attacked

            cols.add(col); diag1.add(row-col); diag2.add(row+col)
            placement.append(col)

            explore(row + 1, placement)   # try next row

            placement.pop()                 # ← backtrack
            cols.remove(col); diag1.remove(row-col); diag2.remove(row+col)

    explore(0, [])
    return solutions

title: Combinatorial Search
text:
What Is Pruning?
Backtracking abandons paths that are already invalid.
Pruning goes further: abandon paths that cannot possibly lead to a valid solution — even if the partial solution is still technically valid.
Backtracking check:

“Is what I’ve done so far broken?”

Pruning check:

“Even if I keep going, can I possibly succeed?”

Pruning is about look-ahead: reasoning about the future before you explore it.

title: Combinatorial Search
text:
Pruning in Subset Sum
Recall subset_sum([3, 1, 4, 2, 5], target=6).
Sort the array first: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
Now we can prune aggressively:
def explore(index, current_sum, chosen):
    if current_sum == target:
        results.append(chosen[:])
        return

    for i in range(index, len(nums)):
        # PRUNING: if even the smallest remaining element
        # pushes us over the target, stop immediately
        if current_sum + nums[i] > target:
            break   # ← all subsequent are even larger (sorted!)

        chosen.append(nums[i])
        explore(i + 1, current_sum + nums[i], chosen)
        chosen.pop()
One extra line. Huge reduction in explored nodes.

title: Combinatorial Search
text:
Pruning: The “Promising” Check
A standard pattern is to add a is_promising() check before recursing:
def explore(partial_solution):
    if not is_promising(partial_solution):  # ← prune here
        return

    if is_complete(partial_solution):
        record(partial_solution)
        return

    for choice in get_choices(partial_solution):
        partial_solution.add(choice)
        explore(partial_solution)
        partial_solution.remove(choice)
is_promising() encodes domain knowledge:
“Given what I’ve committed to, is there any way to finish successfully?”

title: Combinatorial Search
text:
Example: Sudoku with Constraint Propagation
Problem: Fill a 9×9 grid so each row, column, and 3×3 box contains digits 1–9.
. 5 . | . . . | . 7 .
. . 8 | . . . | 4 . .
. . . | 9 . 3 | . . .
------+-------+------
...   (81 cells total)
Backtracking alone: pick an empty cell, try digits 1–9.
Pruning idea: before placing a digit, compute the set of still-possible values for every empty cell.
After placing one digit, you can immediately eliminate many candidates in the same row/column/box.
If any cell’s candidate set becomes empty → prune immediately, no need to recurse.

title: Combinatorial Search
Combinatorial Search
text:
Complexity Analysis
Backtracking doesn’t change the worst case (still exponential), but it dramatically improves average case.



Algorithm
Worst Case
Typical Case




Brute force
O(n!) or O(kⁿ)
Same


Backtracking
O(n!) or O(kⁿ)
Much better


Backtracking + pruning
O(n!) or O(kⁿ)
Far better




The key metric is nodes explored, not theoretical complexity class. Good pruning can reduce explored nodes from billions to thousands.

title: Combinatorial Search
text:
Key Design Decisions
When implementing backtracking + pruning, ask yourself:

What is a “partial solution”?
→ What state do I need to track?
What are the choices at each step?
→ What’s the branching factor?
What makes a partial solution invalid? (backtracking)
→ Implement this check cheaply
What makes a partial solution hopeless? (pruning)
→ This is where domain knowledge pays off
Can I order choices to fail fast?
→ Try the most constrained variables first (MRV heuristic)
